student_message: Yeah yeah. Square has four spins. Next!
final_message: Earth to my speedy math explorer! 🚀 You already know about spinning squares, so let's officially unlock your next math superpower called rotational symmetry. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being turned by a fraction of a full turn around its center point. We usually check fractions like a $$1/4$$ turn, a $$1/2$$ turn, and a $$3/4$$ turn. A full turn does not count, and you can always track the spins by imagining a tiny dot on the shape to see where it ends up! Let's test your super speed with a riddle about a playful toy. I catch the wind and love to spin, and I am Lali's firki, a little pinwheel with four identical blades. At which fractional turns does the firki look exactly the same as its starting position? 🌀

orchestrator_intent.goal: introduce the concept of rotational symmetry and challenge with a quiz.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Section is not started, requiring an introduction. The student is highly confident, rushing, and looking away, so engaging them immediately with a quiz will validate their knowledge and regain focus.

current_section_content: A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated (turned) by a fraction of a full turn around its centre point. The fractions we check are 1/4 turn, 1/2 turn, and 3/4 turn. A shape that only looks the same after a full turn does not count as having rotational symmetry. You can track this by placing a dot on the shape and observing where the dot ends up after each turn.
session_summary: The session covered vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry. The student understood the concepts perfectly, easily identifying the two lines of symmetry in the uppercase letter 'H'. While the student did not struggle mathematically, they initially exhibited slight impatience and frustration with the pacing. The student's current learning state is highly confident, energetic, and eager to advance to the next chapter, demonstrating strong mastery of the current symmetry material.
